title: Support signed quadlets
body:
On the automotive side (and also often in edge etc), we are interested in running validated read-only system images. Typically the way such systems work is by having /etc be a transient version of the image (via an overlayfs with tmpfs backing). This way we can do things like create /etc/resolv.conf during runtime, but an attacker cannot persist any changes to the next boot.

However, we would like to extend this to using a read-only system image with the ability to install container from a curated set., sort of like an app store model. We want these apps to be containers, using quadlet to install into the system. To do this, we want to add an extra writable directory like `/var/extra-containers` (example name), which quadlet reads in addition to the current ones (which come from the read-only system image).  To ensure these come from the curated app list we want the quadlets to be signed, and for quadlet to only accept files that are signed by a trusted public key.

This PR adds support for a quadlet config file that lest you configure extra directories, as well as per-directory signature requirements. See the individual commits for details.

```release-note
Adds ability to sign quadlet files and add custom quadlet source directories
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **NOT APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *alexlarsson* **Once this PR has been reviewed and has the lgtm label**, please assign [baude](https://github.com/baude) for approval. The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman).
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - **[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)** Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

This PR is quite surprising as the topic has not been discussed at all in the bi-weekly meetings.

I miss pretty much all necessary information and background to review this properly.  What is the exact use case?  The analogy with app store is interesting, but I wonder which alternatives to this implementation/idea have been considered.

Couldn´t Quadlets be shipped/installed via RPMs?  Or even as OCI artifacts where we could make use of existing container signing?

@rhatdan @ygalblum PTAL

> Couldn´t Quadlets be shipped/installed via RPMs? Or even as OCI artifacts where we could make use of existing container signing?

I'm not sure what this means in this case. Our entire system is essentially read-only except /var, and is not based on rpm. Also, we don't want to validate at install time, but at boot time, and the thing we want to validate is the ability to launch things.

Lets set up a meeting where we can discuss this.

> the thing we want to validate is the ability to launch things.

Lemme be more detailed about this. We don't want the ability for someone to persist any file that affects boot in an uncontrolled way. For example, if you can write to /etc you can write a service file there that starts a rootkit at boot. Same with a quadlet file, you can put PreExec=/var/my/rootkit in it and persist an attack.

So, we cannot allow any file that systemd reads by default to be writable in an uncontrolled fashion.

Initially I wanted to use an standalone generator to handle this for service files. I.e. one that just copies along the source file once validated. However, that doesn't really work with quadlet, because it is already a generator, and we don't have dependencies or ordering for generators.
Hmm, i guess a separate generator could launch the quadlet generator by itself though...
Thanks!  Looking forward to the conversation :+1:

> Hmm, i guess a separate generator could launch the quadlet generator by itself though...

I'll have a quick pass at this alternative before todays meeting.

> > the thing we want to validate is the ability to launch things.
> 
> Lemme be more detailed about this. We don't want the ability for someone to persist any file that affects boot in an uncontrolled way. For example, if you can write to /etc you can write a service file there that starts a rootkit at boot. Same with a quadlet file, you can put PreExec=/var/my/rootkit in it and persist an attack.
> 
> So, we cannot allow any file that systemd reads by default to be writable in an uncontrolled fashion.

That makes sense to me, but then I struggle with asking what makes Quadlet special? All other systemd units need to be protected the same way. Similarly `/etc/nsswitch.conf` (ability to inject a library into most processes). And `/etc/profile.d`, I’m sure there are dozens if not hundreds of other files in `/etc` that could be similarly abused — generally the old UNIX design is that as long as a config file is writable only by root, it is trusted and doesn’t need any extra validation.

At a first glance it seems to me that such a locked-down system needs to have _everything_ read-only, in particular _all_ of `/etc` by default, except for a specific list of _individually_ allowed exceptions. (And that might still be very hard — if currently “is service $foo enabled” is not a file which could be added to such an exception list, but a presence of an _arbitrarily-named_ symlink pointing at that service … I don’t know what to tell you.)

> That makes sense to me, but then I struggle with asking what makes Quadlet special? All other systemd units need to be protected the same way. Similarly `/etc/nsswitch.conf` (ability to inject a library into most processes). And `/etc/profile.d`, I’m sure there are dozens if not hundreds of other files in `/etc` that could be similarly abused — generally the old UNIX design is that as long as a config file is writable only by root, it is trusted and doesn’t need any extra validation.
> 
> At a first glance it seems to me that such a locked-down system needs to have _everything_ read-only, in particular _all_ of `/etc` by default, except for a specific list of _individually_ allowed exceptions. (And that might still be very hard — if currently “is service $foo enabled” is not a file which could be added to such an exception list, but a presence of an _arbitrarily-named_ symlink pointing at that service … I don’t know what to tell you.)

Indeed, our design is such that all the system files, including the ones in /etc, are read only. And not only read-only, they are also protected from accidental (or deliberate) tampering using composefs. The setup with ostree, composefs , etc is sort of complex, but an equivalent setup would be:

 * system partition is a read-only erofs filesystem with dm-verity to validate all reads. This contains the basic /usr and /etc
 * An extra ext4 partition that is mounted at /var
 * The kernel and initramfs are signed and verified by secure boot
 * The signed initrd mounts /etc as an overlayfs of the system /etc and an upper in /run/etc
 * The initrd validates the toplevel digest of the system partition

A setup like this is nice in that it is 100% identical on all deployments and is verified at runtime. However, it is also very static. We want to allow it to also install extra services, from a set of validated, signed optional extra "apps". These are containers, which we install into /var. But they also come with quadlet file so that they can be started at boot.

This is why quadlet is special. Although, we might also want to support non-quadlet service files in a similar way.

As an alternative approach I wrote a C based version of this:
https://github.com/alexlarsson/validate-generator

It supports validating both regular service files as well as quadlets.
The regular files are just copied to the real target directory after validation. The quadlet files are all copied to a temporary directory, and then the real quadlet generator is launched on that temporary directory, converting the validated files into the real target.

Maybe this is a better approach than including this in podman. Lets talk in the meeting.

> These are containers, which we install into /var. But they also come with quadlet file so that they can be started at boot.

How will the files be installed?

> > These are containers, which we install into /var. But they also come with quadlet file so that they can be started at boot.
> 
> How will the files be installed?

There is an entire subsystem that allows the user to install apps from an appstore, which will download the required files and put them in the right places when they user requests so.

We discussed this in the meeting, and the decision was that while this is tangentially related to quadlet, it is better done as an external project that uses unmodified quadlet as needed.

So, closing this.
